elements.sent-2411-001: Effectively modeling the interaction between human hands and objects is challenging due to the complex physical constraints and the requirement for high generation efficiency in applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: Prior approaches often employ computationally intensive two-stage approaches, which first generate an intermediate representation, such as contact maps, followed by an iterative optimization procedure that updates hand meshes to capture the hand-object relation.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, due to the high computation complexity during the optimization stage, such strategies often suffer from low efficiency in inference.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this limitation, this work introduces a novel diffusion-model-based approach that generates the grasping pose in a one-stage manner.
elements.sent-2411-005: This allows us to significantly improve generation speed and the diversity of generated hand poses.
elements.sent-2411-006: In particular, we develop a Latent Diffusion Model with an Adaptation Module for object-conditioned hand pose generation and a contact-aware loss to enforce the physical constraints between hands and objects.
elements.sent-2411-007: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves faster inference, higher diversity, and superior pose quality than state-of-the-art approaches.
elements.sent-2411-009: The problem of modeling hand-object interactions~\cite{intro-hand-object-interaction-1,intro-hand-object-interaction-2,Doosti2020HOPENetAG,Liu2021SemiSupervised3H,Chen2023gSDFGS,Motion-Generation-for-Hand-Object-Interaction has attracted increasing research interest recently, with important applications in virtual reality~\cite{Costabile2005HumancomputerI , human-computer interaction~\cite{Hll2018EfficientPI,Farulla2016VisionBasedPE , and imitation learning in robotics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: A key task in hand-object interaction modeling is to predict various ways a human hand can grasp a given object.
elements.sent-2411-011: Unlike robot grasping with parallel jaw grippers, the task of predicting human grasps is particularly challenging due to two reasons: First, human hands have more degrees of freedom, resulting in more intricate contact patterns; Moreover, the generated grasp must be not only physically plausible but also appear natural, reflecting the typical ways that humans handle objects.
elements.sent-2411-012: Previous methods for synthesizing human grasping postures often rely on a two-stage process~\cite{gf,halo,liu2023contactgen,tta .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Such a process typically first uses a generative model, e.g., Conditional Variational AutoEncoder (CVAE)~\cite{cvae , to generate a series of intermediate representations, including contact maps~\cite{tta and/or parts maps~\cite{liu2023contactgen , based on the point cloud representation of interacting objects.}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The second stage then uses those intermediate representations to estimate the hand parameters, aiming to produce a natural and physically plausible hand pose.
elements.sent-2411-015: To achieve this, most methods formulate the estimation as an optimization problem and adopt an iterative procedure to search the target hand pose.
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite their promising results, such two-stage methods often suffer from two drawbacks: First, the iterative optimization procedures are computationally intensive, leading to a low inference efficiency and time-consuming generation; Second, the quality of generated hand poses highly relies on the intermediate representations from the first stage and prone to accumulated errors.
elements.sent-2411-017: To address those limitations, we propose an efficient one-stage generation method, named FastGrasp, to directly generate grasping poses without producing intermediate representations like contact maps, while maintaining the diversity of generated poses.
elements.sent-2411-018: To achieve this, we leverage the latent diffusion model framework~\cite{Preechakul2021DiffusionAT to learn a contact-aware representation for hand poses in a latent space and a diffusion-based generation process, capable of better encoding the physical constraints and capturing the object-conditioned hand-pose distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Specifically, FastGrasp first learns a low-dimensional latent representation of hand pose parameters based on an AutoEncoder (AE) network.
elements.sent-2411-020: It then encodes the object with a Point-Net and builds a diffusion model in the latent space conditioned on the object representation.
elements.sent-2411-021: Subsequently, to incorporate the physical constraints on hand-pose interaction, FastGrasp introduces an adaptation module, which refines the diffusion-generated latent representation based on the object contact information.
elements.sent-2411-022: Finally, the contact-aware hand-pose presentation is decoded into the MANO~\cite{mano parameters of the grasping hand pose with the AE decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-023: We validate our approach through extensive experiments on three hand-object interaction benchmarks: HO-3D~\cite{ho3d , OakInk~\cite{YangCVPR2022OakInk , and Grab~\cite{grab .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves low latency in inference and generates higher-quality grasping poses with more plausible physical interactions and higher diversity than recent state-of-the-art approaches.
elements.sent-2411-025: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-026: We introduce FastGrasp, a diffusion-based one-stage model for generating grasping hand pose without requiring expensive iterative optimization.
elements.sent-2411-027: We propose an adaptation module to effectively incorporate physical constraints into a latent hand representation.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our approach achieves fast inference and outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods on a range of metrics.
elements.sent-2411-029: Generating whole-body interactions, such as approaching and manipulating static~\cite{Kulkarni2023NIFTYNO,wu2022saga and dynamic objects~\cite{Ghosh2022IMoSIF , is a growing topic.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The task of synthesizing humans interacting with dynamic objects is explored using first-person vision~\cite{Liu2019ForecastingHO in skeleton-based datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, numerous studies begin to explore hand-object interactions across diverse settings~\cite{Brahmbhatt2019ContactDBAA,Brahmbhatt2020ContactPoseAD,Chao2021DexYCBAB,Liu2021SemiSupervised3H .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Most current efforts focus on synthesizing these interactions in the domains of computer graphics~\cite{Li2007DataDrivenGS,Pollard2005PhysicallyBG,Zhang2021ManipNet , computer vision~\cite{Grady2021ContactOptOC,Jiang2021HandObjectCC,Kry2005InteractionCA,Li2023TaskOrientedHI,Ye2023AffordanceDS,Zheng2023CAMSCM,Zhou2022TOCHSO , and robotics~\cite{Brahmbhatt2019ContactGraspFM,Detry2010RefiningGA,Hsiao2006ImitationLO .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: To perform hand-object pose estimation, Tekin \etal.~\cite{Tekin2019HOUE proposes a 3D detection framework that predicts hand-object poses using two output grids without explicitly modeling their interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In contrast, Hasson \etal.~\cite{Hasson2019LearningJR utilize hand-centric physical constraints to model hand-object interactions and prevent penetration.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Recently, research shifts towards generating plausible hand grasps for objects, with significant contributions including:~\cite{Corona2020GanHandPH,grab .}
elements.sent-2411-036: GanHand~\cite{Corona2020GanHandPH generates grasps suitable for each object in a given RGB image by predicting a grasp type from grasp taxonomy and its initial orientation, then optimizing for better contact with the object.}
elements.sent-2411-037: GrabNet ~\cite{grab represents 3D objects using Basis Point Set to generate MANO~\cite{mano parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The predicted hand is refined using an additional model to enhance contact accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our diffusion-model-based pipeline directly generates the grasping pose for a given object point cloud, eliminating the need for additional models.
elements.sent-2411-040: Grasp synthesis receives extensive attention across robotic hand manipulation, animation, digital human synthesis, and physical motion control~\cite{wu2022saga,wxf .}
elements.sent-2411-041: In this work, we focus on realistic human grasp synthesis~\cite{gf,grab,halo,liu2023contactgen,tta , aiming to generate authentic human grasps for diverse objects.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The key challenge is achieving physical plausibility and generation efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-043: Most existing approaches employ CVAE to generate hand MANO parameters~\cite{grab,tta,YangCVPR2022OakInk or hand joints~\cite{halo .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Liu \etal.~\cite{liu2023contactgen propose learning intermediate representations followed by iterative optimization in two stages.}
elements.sent-2411-045: This method weakens the spatial information of objects, causing intersection penetration and displacement, and requires significant time for optimization in the second stage.
elements.sent-2411-046: In contrast, we develop an one-stage generation model that supervises the spatial geometry of objects and adaptively learns the physical constraints of hand-object interaction.
elements.sent-2411-047: Such model architecture effectively accelerates generation speed and reduces hand-object penetration volume.
elements.figure-2411-001: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0pt} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/train_pipeline.png} \caption{Model training architecture. We divide the training process into two parts. In the first part, we use a latent diffusion model to generate grasping poses from object point clouds. However, the diffusion model struggles to directly learn the physical constraints between the hand and object, leading to issues such as penetration and displacement. To address this, the second part involves training an Adaptation Module to refine the grasping gestures by aligning them with the physical constraints of hand-object interactions, resulting in more natural and feasible poses. In training stage one, only the solid arrow path is utilized. In stage two, both the solid and dotted arrow paths are used.} \label{fig:train-pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-048: Denoising diffusion models~\cite{ho2020denoising,SohlDickstein2015DeepUL,Liu2022CompositionalVG,Poole2022DreamFusionTU,Watson2022NovelVS utilize a stochastic diffusion process that incrementally introduces noise into a sample from the data distribution, adhering to thermodynamic principles.}
elements.sent-2411-049: They then generate denoised samples through a reverse iterative procedure.
elements.sent-2411-050: However, directly training DDPMs on high-resolution point clouds and sampling from them is computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-051: Latent diffusion models address this issue by encoding high-resolution images into a low-dimensional latent space~\cite{lyu2023controllable,Kwon2022DiffusionMA,Preechakul2021DiffusionAT before training DDPMs.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Our approach follows this paradigm: we first train an autoencoder in the data space, and then train a DDPM using the encoded samples.
elements.sent-2411-053: Additionally, we designe an Adaptation Module(AM) to adjust the input to the decoder, incorporating hand-object physical constraints into the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-054: Given an object, usually represented by a point cloud, our purpose is to generate a human hand pose for grasping this object.
elements.sent-2411-055: The generated grasping hand pose should be natural and physically correct, securely holding the object in a physically plausible manner.
elements.sent-2411-056: Unlike the existing methods that usually adopt a two-stage design with high computation cost, we propose FastGrasp, a fast grasping hand pose generation pipeline without estimating intermediate representations and iterative optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-057: FastGrasp is a one-stage generation framework consisting of two main modules for generating the grasping hand pose.
elements.sent-2411-058: The first module is based on a latent diffusion model to preserve the diversity of hand poses when intermediate representations like contact maps are absent.
elements.sent-2411-059: Given the latent hand representation generated from the diffusion model, we introduce an adaptation module to enforce the physical constraints of hand-object interaction.
elements.sent-2411-060: This design allows the model to directly learn the spatial relationship between the hand and object point clouds without iterative optimization, resulting in a fast generation of high-quality hand poses.
elements.sent-2411-061: To learn the entire model, we adopt a simple yet effective two-step training strategy.
elements.sent-2411-062: The first step trains the latent diffusion model, which generates an initial representation of the hand poses.
elements.sent-2411-063: Next, we train the adaptation module to refine the hand representation to strengthen the physical constraints of the hand-object interaction.
elements.sent-2411-064: After training, our generation requires only one pass of network inference, thus significantly accelerating grasping hand generation.
elements.sent-2411-065: Below we will first introduce the latent diffusion model module in Sec.~\ref{latent-diffusion , followed by the adaptation module in Sec.~\ref{distribution-adapt-layer .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Finally, the model inference pipeline will be detailed in Sec.~\ref{inference .}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Latent Diffusion Model for Hand Pose
elements.sent-2411-067: To build our Latent Diffusion model~\cite{Kwon2022DiffusionMA for hand pose, we first train an auto-encoder that maps the input hand representation to a latent space.}
elements.sent-2411-068: This allows us to reduce the data dimensionality for the diffusion process and improves the modeling efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-069: In contrast to the original latent diffusion model, where the input and output are exactly the same, we employ an asymmetric design in the auto-encoder for the subsequent conditional generation process.
elements.sent-2411-070: Specifically, the input to our auto-encoder is the vertices of the hand mesh, $h_v \in \mathbb{R ^{778 \times 3}$, which is first processed by a PointNet~\cite{Pointnet and then fed into the encoder block.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: This design maintains the spatial shape information of the input hand in feature extraction, which can be more easily integrated with the object representation in the later stage.
elements.sent-2411-072: The obtained latent vector is converted to MANO~\cite{mano parameters representation $h_p \in \mathbb{R ^{61}$ instead of the vertices by the decoder block.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The MANO parameters have far less freedom than those of vertices, thus improving the regularization in learning the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-074: The hand mesh vertices $h_m \in \mathbb{R ^{778 \times 3}$ is finally reconstructed from $h_p$ by a differentiable MANO layer~\cite{mano .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The training objective of the AutoEncoder combines a hybrid reconstruction loss and a set of physical constraints.
elements.sent-2411-076: The reconstruction loss measures the difference between the reconstructed hand mesh and the ground truth, which includes two terms: \begin{equation}L_{recon} = \lambda_1 L_{param} + \lambda_2L_{mesh} L_{param} = MSE(h_p, h_p^{gt})\label{eq:LOSS-param} L_{mesh} = Chamfer-Dis(h_m, h_m^{gt}) \label{eq:LOSS-mesh}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{L _{param}$ indicates mean squared error loss between predicted $h_p$ and GT hand MANO parameters $h_p^{gt}$, $\mathcal{L _{mesh}$ measures chamfer distance between the predicted hand vertices $h_m$ and the GT hand vertices $h_m^{gt}$.}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}L_{recon} = \lambda_1 L_{param} + \lambda_2L_{mesh} L_{param} = MSE(h_p, h_p^{gt})\label{eq:LOSS-param} L_{mesh} = Chamfer-Dis(h_m, h_m^{gt}) \label{eq:LOSS-mesh}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-077: To learn a hand representation that adheres to physical constraints, we also employ the following three loss functions from~\cite{tta : \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{consist} = \text{Consist}(h_m, h_m^{gt}, o_m) \label{eq:consist}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{cmap} = \text{Contact}(h_m, o_m) \label{eq:cmap}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{penetr} = \text{Penetra}(h_m, o_m) \label{eq:penetr}\end{equation} where $o_m$ denotes the object mesh that we aim to grasp, $\mathcal{L _{consist}$ aims to make the contact region of the predicted hand mesh on the object consistent with that of the GT hand mesh on the object.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: We refer the reader to the Supplementary for details of those loss functions.
elements.sent-2411-079: Our total loss function for training the auto-encoder (the left part in Fig.~\ref{fig:train-pipeline ) can be written as: \begin{equation}L = L_{recon} + \lambda_{3}L_{cmap} + \lambda_{4}L_{penetr} + \lambda_{5}L_{consist} \label{eq:Total-loss}\end{equation} where $\lambda_{3 , \lambda_{4 , \lambda_{5}$ are weight parameters for balancing the physical constraint loss terms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: By integrating physical and reconstruction losses, our model is able to learn the hand mesh and the physical constraints involved in the interaction between the hand and the object.
elements.sent-2411-081: This approach ensures that our auto-encoder effectively encodes the hand vertices and maintains the physical plausibility of the generated mesh.
elements.figure-2411-002: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0pt} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/inference_pipeline.png} \caption{Model inference architecture. We start by inputting Gaussian noise and the object’s point cloud into the model. The diffusion model then generates hand representations in latent space. The Adaptation Module refines these representations, which are then decoded into MANO parameters. Finally, we construct the hand mesh using the MANO layer.} \label{fig:inference-pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-082: We adopt a diffusion model to learn the distribution of the latent hand representation produced by the auto-encoder.
elements.sent-2411-083: The model gradually denoises a normally distributed random variable, which corresponds to learning the reverse process of a fixed Markov Chain~\cite{ho2020denoising,rombach2021highresolution .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Here we train a denoising U-Net to predict the added noises in the diffusion process, as shown in the right part of Fig. \ref{fig:train-pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Specifically, the input of the diffusion model consists of three parts: $z_0, o_p \in R^{N_o \times 3}$ and $t$.
elements.sent-2411-086: The input object point cloud $o_p$, is used as the conditioning information for our diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-087: It is transformed into an embedding using PointNet~\cite{Pointnet , facilitating controllable generation.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The loss function for training the diffusion network can be written as: \begin{equation}L_{LDM}:=\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{E}(h_v),\epsilon\thicksim\mathcal{N}(0,1),t}\Big[\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(z_0^t,P(o_p),t)\|_2^2\Big] \label{eq:diff1}\end{equation} where $\epsilon_\theta(z_0^t,P(o_p),t)$ denotes the conditional denoising U-Net used for training, where $t$ ranges from 1 to $T$, the input $z_0^t$ is the $z_0$ mixed with $\epsilon$, the $P$ denotes the PointNet~\cite{Pointnet .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Through training, the diffusion model learns to reconstruct the hand mesh from Gaussian noise by denoising and decoding.
elements.sent-2411-090: During the training of the diffusion model, directly incorporating physical loss and reconstruction loss lead to oscillations and hampers convergence.
elements.sent-2411-091: We attribute this issue to the diffusion model's difficulty in simultaneously learning the distribution of the $\mathcal{E}$ output and capturing the physical constraints between the hand and the object.
elements.sent-2411-092: Therefore, the generated hand mesh and object may exhibit significant physical penetration and displacement.
elements.sent-2411-093: To address this problem, we decompose the entire training process into a two-step optimization approach.
elements.sent-2411-094: This method not only simplifies the model’s training complexity but also helps better capture the physical constraint relationship between the hand and the object.
elements.sent-2411-095: Specifically, after training the diffusion model, we aim to adjust the physical constraints of hand-object interactions.
elements.sent-2411-096: To retain the knowledge from the previous diffusion model, we introduce an adaptation module $f_{adapt}$ based on a MLP.
elements.sent-2411-097: The diffusion model's output $z_1$, serves as the input to the adaptation module.
elements.sent-2411-098: This module aligns the distribution learned by the diffusion model with the physical constraints of hand-object interactions.
elements.sent-2411-099: The specific formula is as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} z_2 = f_{adapt}(z_1) \end{aligned} \label{eqa:adapt_loss}\end{equation} where $z_2 \in R^{N_z}$ , is the output of the adaptation module when given $z_1$ as input.
elements.sent-2411-100: The goal of incorporating hand-object physical constraints is to ensure that the resulting hand mesh achieves natural and realistic grasping postures.
elements.sent-2411-101: However, $z_1$ and $z_2$ do not accurately represent the quality of hand-object interactions in real physical space.
elements.sent-2411-102: Therefore, we first reconstruct $z_1$ and $z_2$ back to the MANO parameters $h_p$, and then use the MANO Layer\cite{mano $f_{mano}$ to reconstruct the hand mesh $h_m$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} h_p = \mathcal{D}(z_1 + z_2) \end{aligned} \label{eqa:Decoder}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} h_m = f_{mano}(h_p) \end{aligned} \label{eqa:mano-layer}\end{equation} Next, we update the adaptation module using the loss function \ref{eq:Total-loss to ensure that the physical constraints of hand-object interactions are accurately aligned.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This training method addresses the challenge of directly learning physical constraints in diffusion models, resulting in more natural grasping poses and minimizing unnecessary physical penetration.
elements.sent-2411-104: Fig. \ref{fig:inference-pipeline illustrates the inference process of our method.}
elements.sent-2411-105: During inference, the initial input consists of noise $u$ sampled from a Gaussian distribution and an object point cloud $o_p$.
elements.sent-2411-106: First, we generate the prior $z_1$ for the hand mesh in the latent space through an $N$-step denoising process~\cite{ddim .}
elements.sent-2411-107: Next, the adaptation module integrates $z_1$ with the object point cloud information to generate $z_2$, as shown in Eq. \ref{eqa:adapt_loss .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Finally, $z_1$ and $z_2$ are combined, and the decoder converts them into MANO parameters $h_p$, which are then used by the MANO layer \cite{mano to produce the hand mesh $h_m$.}
elements.sent-2411-109: This process can be described by the equations \ref{eqa:Decoder and \ref{eqa:mano-layer .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: While using Diffusion Models (DDPM) for generating grasp postures marks a significant advancement over the previous two-stage model, there is still a need to enhance generation speed to meet practical requirements.
elements.sent-2411-111: To address this, we employ DDIM \cite{ddim , which optimizes both speed and quality by adjusting the step size during the denoising process.}
elements.sent-2411-112: This approach enables the rapid generation of grasping poses.
elements.figure-2411-003: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0pt} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|l|ccccc} Dataset Details \begin{tabular}{c} Penetration Volume $\downarrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Simulation Displacement $\downarrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Contact Ratio $\uparrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Entropy $\uparrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Cluster Size $\uparrow$ \end{tabular} OakInk~\cite{YangCVPR2022OakInk} Baseline CVAE model 13.08 1.78 98 2.81 1.12 Original diffusion model 18.34 1.45 98 2.91 5.24 Original diffusion model with physical loss 6.31 3.77 71 2.85 1.58 Our whole pipeline 4.37 1.45 94 2.92 4.96 GRAB~\cite{grab} Baseline CVAE model 12.33 1.94 98 2.62 0.87 Original diffusion model 15.46 1.80 96 2.87 3.06 Original diffusion model with physical loss 8.43 5.24 50 2.84 1.26 Our whole pipeline 1.25 1.67 100 2.93 1.87 HO-3D~\cite{ho3d} Baseline CVAE model 23.17 3.12 100 2.64 0.93 Original diffusion model 16.64 2.18 90 2.87 4.04 Original diffusion model with physical loss 12.73 3.87 62 2.87 1.37 Our whole pipeline 5.23 2.14 98 2.88 3.97 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study results on the GRAB, OakInk, HO-3D datasets~\cite{grab,YangCVPR2022OakInk,ho3d}. The evaluation of the HO-3D is an out-of-domain generalization test, where the model is trained using the GRAB dataset. } \label{tab:ablation-total}
elements.figure-2411-004: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0pt} \begin{minipage}{0.02\textwidth} \raisebox{0.8cm}{\rotatebox{90}{ w/o AM}} \raisebox{0.8cm}{\rotatebox{90}{ AM}} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/ablation-grab.png} \end{minipage} \vrule width 0.5pt \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/ablation-OakInk.png} \end{minipage} \vrule width 0.5pt \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/ablation_ho3d_0.png} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.02\textwidth} \raisebox{0.8cm}{\rotatebox{90}{ w/o AM}} \raisebox{0.8cm}{\rotatebox{90}{ AM}} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/ablation-grab-1.png} \captionsetup{labelformat=empty} \caption*{GRAB~\cite{grab}} \label{fig:ablation-grab} \end{minipage} \vrule width 0.5pt \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/ablation-OakInk-1.png} \captionsetup{labelformat=empty} \caption*{OakInk~\cite{YangCVPR2022OakInk}} \label{fig:ablation-OakInk} \end{minipage} \vrule width 0.5pt \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic/ablation-ho3d-1.png} \captionsetup{labelformat=empty} \caption*{HO-3D~\cite{ho3d}} \label{fig:ablation-ho3d} \end{minipage} \caption{Qualitative comparison between our method and Ours w/o Adaptation Module (AM). Starting from the same random Gaussian noise, we visualize the generated grasps by our whole pipeline (first row) and ours w/o Adaptation Module. For each object, we show two different views for visualization (two columns). This comparison demonstrates that our whole pipeline with AM notably reduces object penetration and produces more realistic grasp poses. } \label{fig:ablation-total}
elements.sent-2411-113: In this section, we evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed framework for object-conditioned hand pose generation.
elements.sent-2411-114: The structure is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-115: We first introduce our benchmarking datasets (Sec.
elements.sent-2411-116: Then, we conduct a model analysis to demonstrate the efficacy of each component in the proposed framework (Sec.~\ref{ablation ).}
elements.sent-2411-117: In what follows, we compare our method with the recent state-of-the-art approaches (Sec.~\ref{experiment ).}
elements.sent-2411-118: Finally, we assess the perceived quality and stability of the generated grasping poses through user studies (Sec.. \ref{user-study ).}
elements.sent-2411-119: For experimental settings, we assess the model’s generalization to new objects using the out-of-domain dataset ~\cite{ho3d .}
elements.sent-2411-120: We also evaluate the physical penetration and grasp firmness of the generated poses with an in-domain setting on the OakInk and GRAB datasets~\cite{YangCVPR2022OakInk, grab .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0pt} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0pt} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|cc|cccc} Method \begin{tabular}{c} Penetration Volume $\downarrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Simulation Displacement $\downarrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Contact Ratio $\uparrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Entropy $\uparrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Cluster Size $\uparrow$ \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} Inference Time $\downarrow$ \end{tabular} GrabNet~\cite{grab} 15.50 2.34 99 2.80 2.06 0.23s GraspTTA~\cite{tta} 7.37 5.34 76 2.70 1.43 6.90s HALO~\cite{halo} 25.84 3.02 97 2.81 4.87 10.42s GF~\cite{gf} 93.01 - 100 2.75 3.44 32.75s ContactGen~\cite{liu2023contactgen} 9.96 2.70 97 2.81 5.04 110.60s $ \text{Ours}^1$ 5.23 2.14 98 2.88 3.97 0.14s $\text{ContactGen}^2$ 14.32 2.41 100 2.84 5.23 110.60s $ \text{Ours}^2$ 12.30 1.44 100 2.88 4.41 0.14s \end{tabular} } \caption{Comparison with previous methods on the HO-3D dataset~\cite{ho3d}, where $\text{Ours}^1$ indicates our model is trained on the GRAB~\cite{grab} dataset following~\cite{gf,liu2023contactgen,halo}, and $\text{Ours}^2$ and $\text{ContactGen}^2$ suggests the corresponding models are trained on the OakInk~\cite{YangCVPR2022OakInk} dataset. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on this out-of-domain dataset, setting new benchmarks with faster inference speeds and the best physical metrics for generated grasps.} \label{tab:HO3D}
elements.sent-2411-121: We conduct experiments using the OakInk~\cite{YangCVPR2022OakInk , GRAB~\cite{grab , and HO-3D~\cite{ho3d datasets, adhering to the experimental protocols outlined in~\cite{halo, liu2023contactgen, YangCVPR2022OakInk .. Specifically, in Sec.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The OakInk and GRAB datasets~\cite{YangCVPR2022OakInk, grab consist of hand-object mesh pairs with hand models parameterized by the MANO~\cite{mano model.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: The GRAB dataset includes real human grasps for 51 objects across 10 subjects, whereas the OakInk dataset features real human grasps for 1,700 objects from 12 subjects.
elements.sent-2411-124: Following~\cite{liu2023contactgen,grab,tta , we also evaluate the model's generalization ability by testing on out-of-domain objects from the HO3D dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Following the prior evaluation protocals~\cite{grab, gf, halo, liu2023contactgen, tta, wu2022saga , we evaluate the generated grasping poses using the following criteria: (1) physical plausibility, (2) stability, (3) diversity, (4) generation speed, and (5) perception score.. Physical Plausibility Assessment.}
elements.sent-2411-126: We evaluate physical plausibility by measuring hand-object mutual penetration volume and contact ratio~\cite{gf, halo, liu2023contactgen, tta .}
elements.sent-2411-127: The penetration volume is calculated by voxelizing the mesh into $1mm^3$ cubes and computing the overlapping voxels.
elements.sent-2411-128: The contact ratio indicates the proportion of the grasps in contact with the object.. Grasp Stability Assessment.
elements.sent-2411-129: Following~\cite{gf,wxf,wu2022saga,grab,liu2023contactgen,tta,YangCVPR2022OakInk , we use a simulator to position the object and the generated grasps.}
elements.sent-2411-130: We then measure the average displacement of the object's center of gravity due to gravity.. Diversity Assessment.
elements.sent-2411-131: We assess the diversity of generated grasps following~\cite{halo,liu2023contactgen .}
elements.sent-2411-132: First, we cluster the grasps into 20 clusters using K-means.
elements.sent-2411-133: Diversity is measured by computing the entropy of cluster assignments and the average cluster size, with higher entropy values and larger cluster sizes indicating greater diversity.
elements.sent-2411-134: Consistent with previous work, K-means clustering~\cite{halo, liu2023contactgen is applied to 3D hand keypoints across all methods.. Generation Speed Assessment.}
elements.sent-2411-135: We randomly select 128 objects from the dataset, generate grasping poses for each object, and calculate the average time required to generate a single pose on an NVIDIA A40 GPU.. Perceptual Score Assessment.
elements.sent-2411-136: We conduct a perceptual study, as described in~\cite{halo,tta , with human participants to evaluate the naturalness of the generated grasps.}
elements.sent-2411-137: During training, we use the Adam optimizer, LR = $1e^{-4}$, $N_z = 768 , N_o = 3000$ and bath size = 256.
elements.sent-2411-138: During training the autoencoder, the loss weights are $\lambda_1 = 0.1, \lambda_2 = 1, \lambda_3 = 1000, \lambda_4 = 10, \lambda_5 =10 $.
elements.sent-2411-139: When training the diffusion model, we freeze the auto-encoder and sample 3000 points from the object mesh $o_m$ as the input point cloud $o_p$.
elements.sent-2411-140: When training the adaptation module, we use the same input point cloud and the loss weights are $\lambda_1^d = 100, \lambda_2^d = 0.1, \lambda_3^d = 1000, \lambda_4^d = 20, \lambda_5^d =0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-141: In this section, we conduct an ablation study to systematically evaluate the contribution of each module to the overall framework performance.
elements.sent-2411-142: This approach clarifies the role and impact of each component before delving into a detailed analysis of the experimental results.. Tab.
elements.sent-2411-143: Conversely, the diffusion model excels in entropy and cluster size but struggles with higher penetration, suggesting difficulties capturing the physical constraints of hand-object interactions.
elements.sent-2411-144: Integrating a physical loss function directly into the diffusion model decreases performance by increasing displacement and reducing grasp robustness, underscoring the challenge of aligning hand representations with physical constraints in latent space.
elements.sent-2411-145: Our Adaptation Module approach effectively combines the diffusion model with physical constraints, achieving reduced penetration and displacement, and significantly improving the accuracy of hand-object interactions.
elements.sent-2411-146: Fig. \ref{fig:ablation-total shows that our Adaptation Module method significantly enhances performance across all three datasets, reducing penetration volume and improving generalization on the out-of-domain HO-3D dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-147: This improvement further demonstrates the Adaptation Module's ability to transform distributions, aligning the generated hand latent vector with natural human expectations.

elements.sent-2411-156: Fig. \ref{fig:comparison demonstrates that our method produces highly plausible object grasping.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Although ContactGen produces more diverse grasps than our method in terms of cluster size, our method archives better results with smaller penetration and greater stability.

elements.sent-2411-165: In this paper, we introduce a one-stage framework for rapid and realistic human grasp generation, eliminating the need for iterative optimization processes common in previous methods.
elements.sent-2411-166: We introduce an adaptation module that aligns the generative model’s output with physical constraints, refining hand representations in the latent space to enhance the accuracy and realism of generated grasps.
elements.sent-2411-167: Consequently, our method accelerates grasp generation, improves physical plausibility, and demonstrates robust generalization across diverse test inputs.
